title: Six-strand challah
subtitle: The plaited Jewish egg bread
excerpt: A 900 g plaited challah made from an enriched egg-and-oil dough, with six strands woven into the traditional braid. The crumb is tender and slightly sweet; the crust glossy from the egg wash. Slice thick and eat fresh, or toast the next day.
sourceNotes: Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg: documents a plaited egg loaf in the chapter on bread, noting the glaze produced by egg white applied before baking. Mrs Rundell, A New System of Domestic Cookery (1806), Project Gutenberg: the enriched bread chapter includes a braided loaf with oil and eggs. The six-strand plaiting sequence described here follows the standard British pattern used in domestic baking manuals of the late nineteenth century: right-outer-over-two, left-outer-under-one-over-two.

content.text: Challah is the enriched plaited loaf of the Jewish Shabbat table, baked with eggs and oil rather than butter or milk. The crumb is silky and faintly sweet; the crust, glazed twice with egg, bakes to a deep brown that is nearly lacquered. A three-strand plait is quicker, but the six-strand version gives a loaf that looks like it means it. The extra strands interlock rather than simply weave, producing a tighter braid and a taller rise.
Active time is about 35 minutes: 10 minutes kneading, a short break to roll the strands, and 5 minutes plaiting. The rest is rising and baking.

items.name: Eggs
items.prepNote: large, 2 for the dough + 1 for the glaze

content.text: Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Beat 2 of the {{eggs}} with the {{rapeseed-oil}} and {{water}}. Pour into the flour and mix to a smooth, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 10 minutes by hand or 7 minutes with a dough hook. Challah dough is softer than a lean dough but should clear the bowl sides by the end of kneading.
Cover the bowl and leave at room temperature for 90 minutes until doubled. Challah rises well because of the sugar feeding the yeast; in a warm kitchen it may be ready in 70 minutes.

Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface and knock it back. Divide into 6 equal pieces (about 105 g each). Roll each piece into a rope about 40 cm long, tapered slightly toward each end. Try to keep the diameter consistent along the length, uneven ropes produce an uneven braid. Lay the six ropes parallel, then pinch them together firmly at the top.
Number the strands 1 to 6 from left to right. The

works by a repeating sequence: take strand 6 (far right) and cross it over strand 1 (far left), placing it in the second position from the left. Renumber 1 to 6. Repeat: strand 6 over strand 1 into second position. Continue until all the dough is used and pinch the ends together firmly. Transfer to a lined baking tray. The braid will look loose. It tightens as it proves.

Cover loosely and leave for 45 minutes until noticeably puffed and the gaps between the strands have closed slightly. Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. Beat the remaining {{eggs}} egg and brush it evenly over the whole loaf, getting into the crevices between strands. If using {{sesame-seeds}}, scatter them now.

Bake for 28 to 30 minutes until deep amber-brown and the base sounds hollow. The egg glaze can catch in the creases, if the top is darkening too fast after 20 minutes, lay a loose piece of foil over it. Cool on a rack for at least 20 minutes before tearing.
